speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, you wanted to go back a little. In the last tape, we had arrived at the camp, but we're going to backtrack, so please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you know, during the part that we just came back from, I was asked or told I should put full emotions into my comments that I'm making. And I would like to make some comments on it, if I may. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, when I am asked by many people, how did you survive, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a basic answer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that also will relate why I'm not so emotional when I relate my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] past, the history of my survival. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that stock comment basically is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a long story, but if you want to put it in a nutshell, on how I survived, I would have to say, you have to picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a 5,000 mile minefield. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's a road that's 5,000 miles long, with mines every foot and a half, three feet wide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why 5,000 miles? 1,000 miles for each of the years, the war years. So you walk these 5,000 miles, and it takes you five years to get there. When you walk, you never step on one of the mines. To put it another way, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what makes you survive? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not necessarily the way I'm going to put it in the order of importance, but in combination of it. Combination between youth, health, fitness, luck, circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: In quick succession, minute after minute, hour after hour, day after day, week after week, month after month, and year after year. And that is how I can say how I survived. Because each minute, each hour, each day is a new and different dangerous episode. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And naturally, the youth helped you not to become so emotional over many of the aspects and the areas that-- of your suffering, let's say, and suffering with that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to think back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the rare and unusual episodes that takes guts and finesse, in plain language, chutzpah. For example, I related earlier that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I escaped the Lodz ghetto, and then I went into another ghetto, a smaller ghetto, that was just created that very day, about 20 kilometers from Lodz. In fact, the name of the town was Szczecin, near Lodz. Then subsequently, I went to Kutno to board a train and to go to Warsaw. And those trains were compartmented into three different areas, between Germans, ethnic Germans, [NON-ENGLISH] and Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I had to figure out which-- where would I go? My Polish, having been born in Germany, was limited. So but the Polacks would have loved nothing better but to grab a Jew and turn them over to the Nazis, even though the Nazis didn't like them. But I wouldn't take any chance, so I figured my best chance to take that trip to Warsaw would go into the German car, where the German soldiers were on. And sure enough, I wound up next to-- sitting between a couple of SS men. And they asked me, 'So hey, young man, where are you going?' Naturally, my German, is-- in those years in particular, was absolutely perfect. So I say, 'I'm going to Warsaw.' They said, 'What are you doing in Warsaw?' I said, 'My-- my father is head of the main railway station in Warsaw, The Hauptbahnhof in Warsaw.' Oh, wonderful. Oh, good, good, yeah. And so on. So they gave me food, chocolate, all kinds of goodies, and apparently, it comes with you. I was nervous, I wasn't sure of myself. So it takes a lot of guts.

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the other areas that I related earlier, for example, when I was doing business outside the ghetto, and I smuggled myself out every day, I went to the most affluent-looking areas, where the well-to-do Poles, or ethnic Germans, or Germans even lived. And those are the houses that are in-- because it was an area where you wanted to sell parsley wholesale and parsley retail. Those spices. And sure enough, I had one customer that-- that had a store, a Pole, in the-- on the ground floor of that relatively new building, one of the few buildings that wasn't destroyed during the blitzkrieg in 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, 'Meet me upstairs. I pay you upstairs. I don't have much money down here.' So I followed him. He was on the top floor, I believe on the 12th floor. Went up with the elevator. And then he paid me, and I walked down. I didn't want to take the elevator. And on the 11th floor of that building, there was an SS headquarters. And as I come down that first flight of stairs, sure enough, there was an SS man. And he tells me, oh, you-- in German it's, [GERMAN SPEECH], but it's slang German.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, 'You-- you-- you Polack, go down.' And I stupidly said to myself, 'But I'm not a Polack.' He said, 'So what are you? Are you a Jew?' I said, 'No.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I spoke German, perfect German. And so he said, [GERMAN], it means documents. And sure enough, I had my-- my-- a document from Germany, when I left Germany. And I changed from where it says Israelite. I changed it to Catholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he looks at it, and then this guy that paid me up, that paid me for the merchandise that I delivered to him, he probably didn't even understand. He asked him, 'Is he a Jew?' He probably didn't even know what he said. All he knew was one word, is [NON-ENGLISH]. And he says no, he knew I wasn't trouble. He says, 'No.' So he said, 'Oh, so-- so how come you say?' I said, 'Look, I speak German, I feel like a German.' And that's how I saved. And in my pocket, I had the armband, the Jewish armband. So these are major or minor episodes which you experience day after day after day in a period of five some odd years. So. So tell me more chutzpah stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, they'll come some-- later on, there'll be some chutzpah stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in any case, so now we're in Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. In a sand pit. Why in a sand pit, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the railway terminated in the sand pit, and that was the dead end, literally. Dead end. And just so one would understand, when I mention Treblinka, there were two Treblinkas. There was a Treblinka I, and there was a Treblinka II. Treblinka I was the penal camp. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners from Treblinka I, in fact, built Treblinka II. But in order to get to Treblinka I, you have to pass Treblinka II first, ironically.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that morning, of course we didn't know, because the cattle cars were closed. We didn't know at all. But when we arrived, I would say maybe 30% of that full transport of about 4,000 people probably died in transit. Never survived. In any case, a good, good share of the people that ended the rail, the cattle cars in Rembertow. Because of the lack of water and food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of lack-- exposure and food and whatever. And some of them, sometimes, there was shooting into the rail cars too, so people died there. Others jumped on the cars, and then they threw them back on the cars dead, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your car, did any shooting happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our car-- but we heard them, no. But there were plenty of dead bodies when we arrived in [NON-ENGLISH]. We-- ultimately, after our arrival, we had to take them out from the cars. So we could see there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody in that railroad car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew some of the people that I worked with in [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any facilities, any buckets?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were absolutely no facility. Basically, maybe under normal circumstances, if people could sit in those cars, maybe it would take 50 people. I would estimate there were probably 200 people to each car. And it was basically standing room only without any water, without any food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was mid summer. It was extremely hot. And somehow, that's why I stated before, how does one survive? Youth, health, luck, circumstances, fitness. Did anyone fall over? What happened if someone fell over?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they fell over, and then somebody would not worry about him when he would worry about oneself because it's survival. I mean, under normal circumstances, you probably would try to help, but you wouldn't obviously not agonize over it that much. And I'll relate a little bit further in that area later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived there. It was early in the morning. Then we were marched up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to an elevated area where the barracks and everything was located. And of course a lot of prisoners already were there. Maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a thousand? And then we learned later on, that that very day, before we arrived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they must have liquidated about a thousand and send them to camp number 2 and exterminated them that day. Shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shooting? How did they exterminate them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're-- they're-- no. They're in-- those states already had gas chambers. They gassed them and so on. And we'll come back to that a little later. So finally, so there were maybe 3,000 people sitting there. We were asked to sit down. Then the selection took place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which naturally, most of the women and children immediately went to the left, and the ones selected that looked fit for work, to the right. And everybody else was sitting. And you were ordered not to stand up. And just sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, after about two hours or so, there was a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beating going on there-- there. If anybody made a wrong move, the SS-- and I know their names, by the name of hobsten fuhrer Schwartz, and Lindeger, and untersturmfuhrer Bravfree and hobsten fuhrer van Eupen, and a variety of others. And Ukrainians were there, which were primarily the guards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there we were sitting, and I believe maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a couple of hundred were sitting there that weren't selected yet. And I sensed it's coming to the end, and they haven't picked you yet. There was one man next to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He also apparently felt the same thing, that it's almost toward the end. And you didn't have to speculate what left and right meant. If didn't go to the right, you for sure would wind up in the gas chamber. And that man next to me got up and there comes this untersturmfuhrer Schwartz, the SS man, lifted his gun and shot him straight through the head, right there in the middle. I see it like today. And this man fell backwards. And I have never realized that one has so much blood. A blood, maybe a-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stream?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stream of blood, maybe as thick as this finger, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came from his forehead as he fell back. And it was gushing. And I-- and the blood was gushing where I was sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the man do to get that bullet?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got up and said, 'I'm strong, I can do this.' And the hobsten fuhrer got annoyed and shot him right there on the spot. Now about three, four minutes later, I knew it was just about the tail end of the selection, and I was still sitting there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got up, and I spoke in German; [GERMAN SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: It means?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the same untersturmfuhrer, he raised his gun towards me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and looked at me and took his gun and waved over here. And he waved me to the working group. So that's one of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thousands upon thousands of different circumstances [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to stay alive. It was now and then later and again and again. You don't worry about tomorrow. You only worry about now. You don't worry about next hour. You worry about this minute and the next five minutes and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once again, my life was saved. And surely enough, I believe, after me, one or two more individuals, and the rest of them were marched to Treblinka II and gassed and exterminated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in those gas chambers. How did you know that there were gas chambers in Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: You just know and you sense. And if you didn't know, if you only speculated when you were sitting there, and when you got there a few days later, for sure you learned what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the column on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain things you just know, you sense, you feel. And there's gossip, and there's conversation, you know, gossip, conversation, when speculation becomes reality. So in any case, so including-- and that Treblinka I, that penal camp, was divided into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four sections. Barrack B-- I don't know why they call it Barrack B, but Barrack B was where the prisoners, the real-- the-- the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the experienced people, the-- the carpenters, the people that had a trade, that had trades that the Germans could use for whatever their need, for whatever they needed to do. These were the people in Barrack B. In fact, there were families, husbands, wives, and children. I would estimate maybe about 200 people. And then there was Barrack A. That's where we were when we arrived, commingled with the others. There must have been about 3,000 workers. These were the black workers that performed the kind of work that the professionals [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wouldn't do because they were to important for the Germans. They needed them for the other reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then there was Barrack [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] C, where the Poles, the Aryans, the Christian Poles, for whatever reason, they had to be there because of some crimes that in the German eyes they had committed, for not-- you know, that they had to-- for the certain contingent of their--

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmers were there. They didn't give enough corn. They didn't deliver enough chickens or pork or whatever, you name it. And another barrack was there, I don't know what, but it just called it. That was by the gypsies. They had about 200 gypsies incarcerated there. So these were the four barracks. Now, the next morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had to-- had to get up early in the morning, and they marched us to the rail, to that sand pit, to board flat bed cars that took us to Malkinia and Ostrow Mazowiecka, respectively, to work on the railroad, to unload coals, to-- to-- to-- to-- to align railroad ties. To Malkinia, it would be about 45 minutes, to Ostrow Mazowiecka would be about two hours from-- from-- from the camp. And I did that, along with another group, for about three weeks. And every day, so and so many hundred went, and so many bodies we brought back, and they immediately-- either they were shot there during work, or they were beaten to death, or they just died from natural causes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that went on for about three weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would estimate it must have been about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] August [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 10, or 12, or 15, or maybe a little earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, we came back from one of our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] daily journeys to-- I think that day we went Ostrow Mazowiecka to [GERMAN]. And as mentioned before, before you get to Treblinka I, you have to pass Treblinka II, which was the extermination camp, which incidentally had their own group of workers that were processing their daily chores. Their work was to collect the clothes and remove the gold and-- and-- and-- and cut, shave the-- the-- the heads, the hair, which was-- everything was collected. I mean, the Nazis made use out of everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about a few hundred yards before we got to that camp II, now we could see. Because the first time when we arrived in Treblinka, we didn't know because the-- the cattle cars were closed, but now they're open. And the train stopped. We would see all kinds of smoke and noise and commotion, stuff like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened that we later on learned, there was an uprising of Treblinka II, which is very famous, which is documented in publications. And in fact, how, in what manner, in what circumstances that Treblinka I and Treblinka II, the people that organized the operation, they were, in fact, aligned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which-- on which I will give you the details in a moment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waited there, and afterwards we learned, as I just mentioned, there was an uprising, and they destroyed the gas chambers and everything. And from what I understand, several hundred people escaped, but most of them were ultimately recaptured and killed on the spot. From what I understand from Treblinka II right now, there are only three or four survivors. From my camp, Treblinka I, there are nine survivors. Actually, now it's only 8 from what I know. Were they all Jews, the people who--?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jews. We're only speaking about Jews. So ultimately, about two hours, then we got a little closer. Then I see the last, must have been, the last transport on that day that came to that-- to Treblinka II for the group of Jews from the Bialystok ghetto arrived that day to be exterminated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remembered just like this very second. I can see it. Because we were-- our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] car, railroad car, was parallel with a cattle car, right-- a few feet over. And I remember one lady shouting down to the Ukrainians, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [POLISH SPEECH] What this means, for the people that don't understand Polish, today it's us, and tomorrow it's you. And you talk about the emotions. When I think about that particular episode, I get goose pimples because I remember it like it would have been just two minutes before. But what we subsequently learned, that group that came from Bialystok, you only can estimate maybe 500, 800 people. And the gas chambers, one no longer functioning, they were shot and burned into the-- in the pit that was also there near Treblinka II. OK. So ultimately, they couldn't do much with us, and, in fact, they needed us. Because when we got back to camp II, we found out there was an uprising there too. And they eliminated just about everybody there, including the kapos that were involved in the uprising and some of the other people that worked in the-- in-- in the offices. A couple of German guys that spoke German, and they worked in the offices for the Germans. And the people from the laundry, from the Jewish laundry, and the people that-- and-- and the water carriers, and so on, They were all involved. In fact they got to the gun [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] storage place. They stole guns and ammunition and [? worse. ?] So that day, they eliminated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody except maybe about 550 people.
